fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that deployment automation toolkit we've been discussing. The core idea is that right now our devs are spending way too much time manually typing out server lists, copy-pasting SSH flags, and debugging why staging got the wrong config values because someone forgot to update a placeholder. It's causing real pain especially around release days.

Basically the tool should let you write something compact like `h[1:20]` instead of listing 20 hostnames, load an inventory file that can have reusable template blocks (the hidden/dot-prefixed ones shouldn't show up in output), and spit out the right SSH connection strings automatically. We also want filtering so you can say 'only run this on prod servers' or 'skip frontend tier'.

Config-wise, it should handle the inheritance thing we talked about — child hosts should be able to pull values from parent scope, and if a parent has a placeholder like `{{name}}` in a path, each child should fill that in with their own name. Also need array config merging but it should scream if you try to append to something that isn't a list.

Finally, task planning — similar to how we handled the hook ordering in that pipeline module a while back — before/after hooks around grouped task expansion, with a clean error if someone references a task that doesn't exist.

We need a test harness that can run against the case files automatically. Python is preferred.

One extra pass on the details the team asked about: for host range expansion, if the bracketed range is zero-padded like `[01:20]`, we need to keep that padding all the way through the generated names, so `01`, `02`, … `09`, `10`, … `20`. The width comes from the length of the start value string, so we shouldn't normalize or strip it during expansion.

Also on inventory loading, any entry whose alias/key starts with `.` is template-only and should stay fully out of the visible host list and all emitted output, even though it can still be used through YAML merge keys like `<<: *anchor` by visible entries. That's the same behavior we want in Feature 1.2 where those dot-prefixed entries are basically reusable anchor templates merged into real hosts.

A couple output formatting specifics too: if a host field is a list/array like `roles`, print it as a compact JSON array string, for example `host.foo.roles=["a","b","c"]`. Regular scalar strings still print as-is, and if `roles` happens to just be a plain scalar string instead of a list, then it should stay a plain string value. For host type, if a host entry has a `local` key at all, no matter what the value is, that host should come out as `type=local`. Every other visible host is `type=remote`, and local hosts should not get `ssh_arguments` output.

For SSH rendering, the order needs to be strict: first bare sshFlags with no value, then forward_agent as `-A` when true, then valued sshFlags as `flag value`, then `-p port`, then `-F config_file`, then `-i identity_file`, then `-o Key=Value` for each sshOptions entry. One subtle point there: if `ssh_multiplexing` is false, we still emit `-A` when `forward_agent` is true, and `ssh_multiplexing` by itself does not add any flag.

On the task planning side, for Feature 5 the before/after hook declarations should expand recursively around grouped task members, matching the `TaskPlanner` domain logic and the behavior shown in `feature5_1_task_execution_plan.json`.

And for the harness, the expected entry point is `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`, with an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` and default `test_cases`. For each case file and case index, it writes actual output to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_3_digit_index>.txt`, for example `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_host_range_expansion@000.txt`, and then compares actual vs expected and reports pass/fail per case.

A few exact details to be precise about: The test harness is invoked via `bash rcb_tests/test.sh` and accepts `--cases-dir <subdir>` (default `test_cases`). For each case file and case index, it writes actual output to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_3_digit_index>.txt` (e.g., `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_host_range_expansion@000.txt`). The script compares actual vs expected output and reports pass/fail per case.

trigger_keywords: merge defaults with overrides
override priority in ssh args
default flags not clobbering explicit values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When merging defaults and overrides for SSH argument rendering: flags from defaults are listed first, then flags from overrides. For options, if the same key appears in both defaults and overrides, the override value wins — the default value is discarded for that key. Flags are always union-appended (defaults then overrides), never deduplicated.

trigger_keywords: hostname from alias slash
deriving hostname from alias
slash separator in host alias
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a host alias contains a forward slash (`/`), the substring before the first slash is used as the default hostname when no explicit `hostname` value is provided. If the alias has no slash, the full alias string is the default hostname.

trigger_keywords: selector all keyword
comma separated predicates
combined label filter logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A selector string is split on commas; each part is an individual predicate. `all` matches every host unconditionally. `key=value` matches hosts where the label `key` equals `value`. `key != value` (with optional spaces around `!=`) excludes hosts where `key` equals `value`. All predicates in a comma-separated list must simultaneously match (logical AND). Hosts are returned in their original input order.

trigger_keywords: placeholder resolution in inherited values
child context for parent template
per-child placeholder substitution
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a child looks up a value defined in a parent or root scope that contains `{{placeholder}}` expressions, those placeholders must be resolved using the requesting child's own configuration values — not the parent's. This means two different children inheriting the same parent template string will each get a differently resolved result based on their own keys.

trigger_keywords: append to missing key creates list
array append to nonexistent config
extending inherited array config
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Appending to a key that does not exist yet creates a new list containing the appended values. Appending to a key that holds a list (whether defined locally or inherited from a parent) extends that list. Appending to a key that holds a scalar (non-list) value must produce `error=config_value_not_array` followed by `key=<key>` and halt processing for that key.

trigger_keywords: before hook expansion order
task group flattening with hooks
hook placement around grouped tasks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When planning a grouped task: (1) collect all `before` hooks targeting that task and expand each hook's task name recursively, (2) expand the target group's member tasks recursively, (3) collect all `after` hooks and expand them. The final execution order is: expanded before-hook tasks, then expanded group members, then expanded after-hook tasks. If the requested plan name does not exist in definitions, output `error=item_not_found` with no further lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

are there public test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature1_2_inventory_loading:
{
  "description": "Loads an inventory document into visible host records, skipping hidden template entries, expanding ranged aliases, preserving local-host markers, and rendering connection-related settings.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "yaml": ".base: &base
  deploy_path: path
  roles:
    - a
    - b
    - c
  param: param

foo:
  <<: *base
  extra: extra

bar:
  hostname: bar.com
  remote_user: remote_user
  port: 22
  config_file: configFile
  identity_file: identityFile
  forward_agent: true
  ssh_multiplexing: false
  sshOptions:
    Option: Value
  sshFlags:
    -f:
    -someFlag: value
  param: param

local:
  local: ""
  deploy_to: /var/local
  param: local

db[1:2].example.org:
  stage: production
  roles: db
"
      },
      "expected_output": "hosts=foo,bar,local,db1.example.org,db2.example.org
host.foo.type=remote
host.foo.hostname=foo
host.foo.roles=["a","b","c"]
host.foo.param=param
host.bar.type=remote
host.bar.hostname=bar.com
host.bar.param=param
host.bar.remote_user=remote_user
host.bar.port=22
host.bar.config_file=configFile
host.bar.identity_file=identityFile
host.bar.forward_agent=true
host.bar.ssh_multiplexing=false
host.bar.ssh_arguments=-f -A -someFlag value -p 22 -F configFile -i identityFile -o Option=Value
host.local.type=local
host.local.hostname=local
host.local.deploy_to=/var/local
host.local.param=local
host.db1.example.org.type=remote
host.db1.example.org.hostname=db1.example.org
host.db1.example.org.roles=db
host.db2.example.org.type=remote
host.db2.example.org.hostname=db2.example.org
host.db2.example.org.roles=db
"
    }
  ]
}

context_pointers.vague_reference: the inheritance thing we talked about
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4.2 inherited configuration: root → parent → child scope chain where placeholder resolution in inherited values uses the requesting child's own config map, implemented in the `ConfigResolver` / inherited configuration module

context_pointers.vague_reference: it should scream if you try to append to something that isn't a list
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4.3 array configuration append: must emit `error=config_value_not_array\nkey=<key>\n` when the target key holds a scalar value; see `feature4_3_array_configuration_append.json`

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` to figure out the exact expected behavior. The test cases are the source of truth for output format.

feature1_1_host_range_expansion.json.description: Expands compact host pattern ranges into the concrete host aliases that a deployment target list should contain, preserving literal hostnames and reporting the resulting order and size.

feature1_2_inventory_loading.json.description: Loads an inventory document into visible host records, skipping hidden template entries, expanding ranged aliases, preserving local-host markers, and rendering connection-related settings.

input.yaml:
.base: &base
  deploy_path: path
  roles:
    - a
    - b
    - c
  param: param

foo:
  <<: *base
  extra: extra

bar:
  hostname: bar.com
  remote_user: remote_user
  port: 22
  config_file: configFile
  identity_file: identityFile
  forward_agent: true
  ssh_multiplexing: false
  sshOptions:
    Option: Value
  sshFlags:
    -f:
    -someFlag: value
  param: param

local:
  local: ""
  deploy_to: /var/local
  param: local

db[1:2].example.org:
  stage: production
  roles: db

cases.expected_output:
hosts=foo,bar,local,db1.example.org,db2.example.org
host.foo.type=remote
host.foo.hostname=foo
host.foo.roles=["a","b","c"]
host.foo.param=param
host.bar.type=remote
host.bar.hostname=bar.com
host.bar.param=param
host.bar.remote_user=remote_user
host.bar.port=22
host.bar.config_file=configFile
host.bar.identity_file=identityFile
host.bar.forward_agent=true
host.bar.ssh_multiplexing=false
host.bar.ssh_arguments=-f -A -someFlag value -p 22 -F configFile -i identityFile -o Option=Value
host.local.type=local
host.local.hostname=local
host.local.deploy_to=/var/local
host.local.param=local
host.db1.example.org.type=remote
host.db1.example.org.hostname=db1.example.org
host.db1.example.org.roles=db
host.db2.example.org.type=remote
host.db2.example.org.hostname=db2.example.org
host.db2.example.org.roles=db

feature2_1_ssh_argument_rendering.json.description: Formats transport flags and key/value options into the command-line argument string used for remote connections, while keeping default values from overriding explicit values.

feature2_2_host_connection_profiles.json.description: Builds host connection profiles from aliases and connection settings, deriving the hostname, user-qualified connection target, and remote transport arguments.

cases.expected_output:
alias=host
hostname=hostname
connection=remote_user@hostname
remote_user=remote_user
port=22
config_file=~/.ssh/config
identity_file=~/.ssh/id_rsa
forward_agent=true
ssh_multiplexing=true
ssh_arguments=-A -p 22 -F ~/.ssh/config -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa -o BatchMode=yes -o Compression=yes

feature3_1_selector_filtering.json.description: Filters an ordered host list by comma-separated label predicates, including matching every host and excluding hosts whose labels equal a given value.

feature4_1_template_resolution.json.description: Resolves placeholder expressions in text values using configuration entries, including default fallback values and computed entries that are evaluated to text before substitution.

feature4_2_inherited_configuration.json.description: Resolves configuration values through parent and root scopes, caching inherited values per child so child-specific placeholders are resolved in the requesting child context.

feature4_3_array_configuration_append.js.description: Appends configuration array values, creating missing arrays, extending arrays inherited from parents, and reporting a normalized error when an append targets a scalar value.

feature5_1_task_execution_plan.json.description: Produces the ordered list of tasks to execute for a requested task name, expanding grouped tasks and placing before and after hooks around the target task.
